title: The Pattern Journal
summary: Peter explains his 40-year habit of journaling patterns

content.opening: You want to know my real secret weapon? It's not a spreadsheet. It's not a Bloomberg terminal. It's a notebook.
body: For forty years—FORTY YEARS—I've kept what I call a Pattern Journal.
Every single day, I write down three patterns I noticed. Could be anything. Lines at a store. How people behaved in a meeting. What happened before a good decision. What happened before a bad one.
Most of them? Useless. I'd say 95% of what I write down goes nowhere.
But that 5%? That 5% contains the insights that changed everything.
The Dunkin' Donuts insight came from the journal. I'd written 'Long line at Dunkin, 6:47 AM, third time this week' over and over before it clicked.
The exercise-spending connection? Journal. 'Skipped gym, bought two things on Amazon I didn't need.' Repeated entries over months.
The journal isn't about being right. It's about NOTICING. Most people walk through life on autopilot. The journal forces me to pay attention.
Here's what I've learned: Patterns exist everywhere. The problem isn't that they're hidden. The problem is we're not looking.
Start writing down what you notice. Three things a day. Do it for a year. You'll start seeing the world differently.
And some of those observations—you won't know which ones at first—will become insights that change how you make decisions.
content.closing: The journal is 47 volumes now. It's the most valuable thing I own. Not for what it's worth—for what it taught me to see.
content.key_insight: Systematic observation—writing down patterns daily—is the foundation of insight. Most observations lead nowhere, but the ones that do change everything.

usage_hints.when_to_use: When discussing methodology, developing observation skills, or how Peter spots patterns
usage_hints.emotional_setup: User curious about how to develop pattern recognition skills
usage_hints.delivery_style: Reflective, personal, building to practical advice
